methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series G → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.25x) to $800.0B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Space & Aerospace (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 22 same-sector peers exist in Space & Aerospace, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Space & Aerospace P/S median is 45.3x across 6 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
SpaceX, a Series G space and aerospace company founded by Elon Musk, has most recently raised capital at an $800B post-money valuation. The Space & Aerospace sector encompasses 5 public comparables (RKLB, PL, LUNR, ASTS, SATL) and 8 private peers including Rocket Lab, Blue Origin, and Planet Labs, though none provide sufficiently dense valuation data at identical or adjacent stages to enable reliable peer-median analysis.

Of three valuation methods tested, only stage-progression proved applicable. Using the company's last post-money valuation of $800B as a baseline and applying a 1.25x step-up multiplier typical for Series G-to-subsequent-stage progression yields an implied valuation of $1.0T. Peer-median anchoring cannot be applied due to insufficient narrow-set comparables at matching stages and valuation tiers within the sector. Public-comp P/S anchoring, while the sector median P/S is 45.3x across six public tickers, cannot be deployed because the target company's revenue is not disclosed.

The single-method triangulation supports a valuation range of $850B to $1.1T, with a median implied value of $1.0T. This range reflects modest upside from the last known round. Key uncertainties include the absence of peer-based corroboration and lack of revenue disclosure, which limits confidence in the upper bound. Given reliance on a single methodology, investors should treat this as a floor estimate pending additional financial transparency or emergence of tighter comparables.
